question_text: Hi IT, something really strange happened after the security changes that were announced recently, and now multiple departments are stuck. First: in Sales, several people can receive emails fine, but anything they send from the desktop mail app just sits in the Outbox and eventually throws a connection-style error. If they use the web mailbox on the browser, sending works. Second: our external project partners are suddenly shown as “Offline” all the time in the chat tool, even while they claim they are online; chats arrive late and calls to them sometimes don’t ring. Third: the multifunction printer says a big scan was “sent successfully”, but when we scan a 20–30 page PDF, nobody receives it (internal or external). Small scans still arrive. Finally: in Basel, the main office printer stopped reacting to print jobs yesterday, and the jobs vanish from the queue as if they never existed, while other printers in the building are fine. People are blaming their laptops, but this looks bigger than that. What is going on? And please confirm, should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: ## Recommendation Classify this ticket as **second_level_support**. ## Reasoning (concise) - You describe simultaneous, cross‑service failures touching mail submission from desktop clients (desktop Outbox fails while web mail works), Teams presence/call failures to external partners, large‑scan SMTP relay failures, and printers/print‑server queue loss. In the indexed corpus the same symptom classes were handled as engineer‑level escalations rather than routine first‑line fixes: Exchange/SMTP AUTH / Conditional Access desktop send failures, Teams federation/presence incidents, Scan‑to‑Mail SMTP relay limits, and print‑server/VM‑restore printer outages all required specialist teams and cross‑team coordination to resolve. These prior incidents required investigator actions beyond standard first‑line playbooks (engineer changes, configuration/template restoration, connector reinstalls, VM/restore and forensic work) [Data: Sources (32, 26, 36, 28, 22)]. ## How the ticket maps to prior incidents (evidence from the records) - Desktop mail clients stuck in Outbox while webmail sends succeed - matches prior Exchange/SMTP AUTH / Conditional Access incidents that required engineering intervention (selective SMTP AUTH/tenant policy adjustments and connector review) [Data: Sources (32)]. - External partners shown "Offline", delayed chats and failing calls - maps to Teams federation/presence and Direct‑Routing incidents that required blueprint‑driven tenant/federation fixes and UC/Network engineer involvement [Data: Sources (26)]. - Large 20-30 page scan sent but not received (small scans OK) - aligns with prior Scan‑to‑Mail incidents where SMTP relay policy or device scan/profile limits caused large messages to be rejected and required Mail Team / relay config changes [Data: Sources (36)]. - Basel printer jobs disappearing from the queue - mirrors print/server incidents where spooler, share‑publishing or VM/host restore issues required infrastructure on‑call and server forensic actions (spooler restarts, VM snapshot/restore investigations) [Data: Sources (28, 22)]. Because multiple domains are affected simultaneously and prior resolutions for these symptom classes required engineer work and cross‑team coordination, the proper classification is second_level_support [Data: Sources (32, 26, 36, 28, 22)]. ## Immediate (recommended) next steps - explicitly marked as recommendations (these are NOT documented as performed in the prior cases) - Recommended: escalate now to second‑level teams (Identity/Entra/AD, Exchange/Mail team, Unified Communications/Direct Routing, Network/Security, Infrastructure/VM/Print owners) and open a multi‑team incident bridge. - Recommended: collect and attach these artifacts (time‑bound around the security change window) for triage: - Exchange/SMTP logs showing outbound 535/error lines and client‑side MAPI/SMTP client errors [recommended]. - Azure AD / Entra sign‑in and Conditional Access evaluation logs for affected senders and for representative users calling/chatting with external partners [recommended]. - Teams/Direct‑Routing/SBC logs and SIP/SDP traces for failed calls; RTP traces if available [recommended]. - SMTP relay logs (scan‑to‑mail) showing message‑size rejects and device MFP logs for the large scans [recommended]. - Print‑server spooler logs, Windows EventLog, and hypervisor/restore job IDs (if a VM restore was performed) for the Basel printer hosts [recommended]. (These artifact requests are operational recommendations informed by the prior incidents; the indexed records show these artifact types were decisive in similar past cases but do not contain your environment's actual files) [Data: Sources (32, 26, 36, 28)]. ## Short justification to hand to your ops lead - Multi‑domain failure pattern + coincidence with recent security changes = high likelihood of a configuration/regression or perimeter/identity policy change with cross‑service impact. Prior cases with this pattern required engineer escalation (second level) to modify tenant policies, restore templates, adjust relay/firewall rules, or perform server restores [Data: Sources (32, 26, 36, 28, 22)]. ## Key supporting references (top, from the indexed knowledge base) - Exchange / SMTP AUTH / desktop Outbox escalation examples [Data: Sources (32)]. - Teams federation / presence and Direct‑Routing incidents requiring blueprint fixes [Data: Sources (26)]. - Scan‑to‑Mail / SMTP relay size‑reject diagnostics and remediation [Data: Sources (36)]. - Print‑server / SAP printing and VM‑restore incidents where spooler/restore/BSOD forensic work was needed [Data: Sources (28)]. - Site printer and queue incidents (printer jobs vanishing; network/VLAN issues) [Data: Sources (22)]. If you want, I can now (recommended) assemble a one‑page evidence request (ticket text) you can send to the owners of Mail, Identity, UC, Network and Infrastructure that lists the exact artifacts and time windows to collect - mark that as requested and I will produce it.

question_text: URGENT SECURITY + ACCESS INCIDENT: Since yesterday afternoon, multiple things broke right after a suspected phishing event. 1) Several employees opened what looked like normal PDF downloads, but then they were redirected to a very convincing Microsoft-style login page. Security now assumes credentials may have been harvested. 2) As an emergency reaction, IT tried to force all non-MFA logins through a country restriction, but now legitimate users traveling are blocked and business-critical work is stopped. 3) At the same time, a few critical automated accounts (backup and an integration service user) started failing to sign in and scheduled jobs broke, but we’re afraid to loosen the security controls. 4) And one internal integration suddenly cannot authenticate anymore even though the same account still works in the browser UI — the automation fails only in the background connection. People are blaming passwords and laptops, but this all started together. What is going on, and please confirm — should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Recommendation - classification - second_level_support What the indexed records explicitly document (relevant precedents) - A confirmed phishing/credential‑harvest incident where investigators found convincing fake Microsoft login pages and recommended emergency containment (geo/firewall controls) and account exceptions. The ticket records the phishing artifacts, the emergency mitigation proposal, and the need to carve out exceptions for critical accounts [This exact workflow is recorded in the phishing incident ticket] [Data: Sources (4)]. - A case where an integration stopped authenticating in background while the same account still worked interactively in a browser: investigators found ADFS / client‑registration parameters were misconfigured (EnableJWT / RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) and that change broke non‑browser/native automation while leaving interactive browser logins functional [Data: Sources (11)]. - A tenant case where OWA (browser) continued to work but non‑browser SMTP/legacy clients failed because Conditional Access / SMTP AUTH policy interactions required scoped exceptions to restore service - this was handled by identity/mail teams and required policy adjustments rather than only helpdesk steps [Data: Sources (32)]. - Cases where connector/automation failures (NDES/other connectors) and immediate network/configuration mitigations required multi‑team (identity, network, security) remediation and on‑call engineering activity, not simple first‑line fixes [Data: Sources (35)]. Why this ticket matches second_level_support - Multiple independent subsystems failed simultaneously after a suspected phishing event: (a) credential compromise risk and emergency CA/geolocation changes that now block legitimate traveling users, (b) automated/service accounts failing, and (c) one integration where interactive browser auth still works but background/auth automation fails. The indexed incidents above show that this pattern (phishing -> emergency containment changes -> legitimate user/service disruption -> non‑browser/integration auth failures) required coordinated engineer involvement (identity/AD/ADFS, Conditional Access, network/firewall, and application owners) to diagnose and safely remediate [Data: Sources (4); Sources (11); Sources (32); Sources (35)]. - Prior tickets with the same set of symptoms were not resolved by routine first‑line steps: they required identity‑plane diagnostics (Azure AD/ADFS token/claim checks, provisioning/Client registration review), policy edits with careful exception scoping, and network/config changes that needed system engineers - i.e., second‑level work [Data: Sources (11); Sources (32); Sources (35)]. What the records do NOT prove about your live incident - The indexed records show how similar events behaved and were resolved, but they do not contain your tenant's live logs, firewall change timestamps, Conditional Access application timestamp, or confirmation that credentials were actually exfiltrated in your environment. The exact root cause in your case cannot be confirmed from the indexed corpus alone [Data: Sources (4)]. Recommended next actions (these are recommendations - not extracted as performed in the records) - Escalate now to second_level_support / Identity & Security engineering and open a cross‑functional bridge between Identity, Network/Firewall, and the application/integration owners (recommended). - Collect and attach the high‑value evidence for engineers: Azure AD sign‑in logs for affected accounts, Conditional Access evaluation logs and change history, firewall/CAB change timestamps for the geo/block rule, list of impacted service/integration accounts and their error traces, and a sample integration failure trace showing interactive vs non‑interactive auth behavior (recommended). Concise rationale to present to your ops lead - This is a multi‑domain, high‑impact incident that matches prior cases (phishing -> emergency CA/geoblocking -> legitimate user/service disruption -> non‑browser auth break) that required identity and network engineers to diagnose and safely remediate; classify as second_level_support and escalate immediately [Data: Sources (4); Sources (11); Sources (32); Sources (35)]. If you want - I can draft the short escalation note and the exact list of logs/artifacts the identity/engineer team will need (recommended).

question_text: Hi Helpdesk… sorry for the chaos but we’re really blocked and it’s all happening at the same time. In SharePoint, the project area for the 2025 migration is basically ‘walled off’ now. The site itself loads, other project spaces open, but THIS one folder/path just throws that classic “you don’t have rights / not allowed” message for a bunch of us who were working in it literally yesterday. Separately (maybe related?), Compliance did some housekeeping: they renamed a folder and slapped one of those ‘Confidential’ / lock-type classifications on it (you know the banner at the top with the label). Since then, access is totally inconsistent. Some people can open files, others get blocked, and one colleague claims it only works if he opens it in a private/incognito browser window. Same user, same link, different result depending on how he opens it… super weird. Also our external auditor can’t deliver their nightly package to our ‘secure drop’ anymore. From their side it just sits there and then times out. On our side it *looks* like the server exists and is up. We even tried WinSCP to reproduce and it behaves the same (hang → fail). They swear they didn’t change anything. While trying to coordinate all this in Teams, one key person’s webcam basically vanished. In Teams settings there’s no camera option at all (not even a dropdown), and even Teams in the browser can’t see a camera. But Windows still lists a camera device in Device Manager, so it doesn’t feel like the hardware is dead. And during the same Teams call, another person can hear everyone perfectly, but nobody can hear them. Teams shows a mic selected, but the little input bar never moves (like it’s stuck at zero). They’re on a USB headset. We already rebooted, swapped USB ports, tried a different browser, and did the usual ‘turn it off and on again’ stuff. Is this connected or just a cursed Monday? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Recommendation: second_level_support Short rationale - The ticket describes simultaneous, cross‑domain failures: a single SharePoint folder suddenly denying access (after a folder rename + sensitivity label), inconsistent access that depends on browser session/token state, an external auditor's SFTP/WinSCP transfer failing, and Teams device (camera + mic) problems during the same window. In the indexed corpus, that pattern - identity/group/label changes producing downstream effects across SharePoint, provisioning, and other services - was repeatedly escalated to identity/system engineers rather than resolved by routine first‑line fixes. [Data: Sources (13); Sources (30); Sources (20)] What the records explicitly document (relevant precedents) - Cloud vs on‑prem group/object collisions (same display name, different ObjectIDs) can exclude cloud guests and cause SharePoint folder access to break; remediation required group consolidation and a full Azure AD Connect sync and was performed by identity/system engineers (example: COMPLIANCE_TEAM case). [Data: Sources (13)] - Sensitivity‑label vs ACL mismatches (label referencing a different group than the folder ACL) caused exactly the inconsistent "some users can open it, others denied / Incognito works" symptoms; prior remediation included re‑aligning label -> group, reapplying the label and invalidating tokens - actions handled by identity/Compliance and engineering teams. [Data: Sources (30)] - Project‑folder nesting errors (SP_Project_IT / SP_Project_IT_v1) required correcting group nesting and were not simple single‑step helpdesk fixes in the records. [Data: Sources (20)] - Provisioning automation / script changes have caused downstream, non‑local symptoms (e.g., voice routing misassignment); when provisioning or sync logic is implicated investigators escalated to the Identity/provisioning owners and engineers. That shows cross‑system automation defects historically require second‑level involvement. [Data: Sources (9)] - Token caching / Conditional Access timing produced intermittent access until tokens were refreshed; sign‑in logs and token claims were used by engineers to prove and fix these behaviours. [Data: Sources (38)] Why this maps to second_level_support - The incident spans the identity/control plane (labels, group/object identity, token claims) and endpoint/media plane (Teams camera/mic). Prior tickets with the same multi‑vector signature required identity engineering (Azure AD / Entra ID / AD Connect / Purview) plus cross‑team coordination (M365/Compliance, network/transport, Unified Communications). These are beyond standard runbook desktop fixes and were handled by system engineers in the indexed cases. [Data: Sources (13); Sources (30); Sources (9)] What the indexed records do NOT show - The records do not (and cannot from here) prove causality for this specific, live ticket (i.e., I cannot confirm whether a single org change or a sync ran "last night" in your tenant). The citations above are precedent cases showing what was required when similar symptoms occurred, not a direct forensic readout of your environment. [Data: Sources (13); Sources (30)] Recommended next steps (these are recommendations - not claims from the records) - Collect and attach the following artifacts into the escalation (Identity / M365 / System Engineers and Security): 1) SharePoint folder permission view and the applied Sensitivity Label configuration for the affected folder (show which group/object each references). 2) Azure AD group object exports (displayName + objectId + onPremises source) for the groups referenced by the folder/label. 3) Azure AD sign‑in log entries for a few affected users, including token claim snippets and CA evaluation results (timestamps). 4) A sample WinSCP/SFTP transfer attempt trace from the auditor (timestamps + server‑side logs) showing the failure. 5) Teams diagnostics from affected clients (desktop logs, Test Call results, and the affected user's Teams client logs) and a note that Device Manager shows the camera present. (These collection steps mirror the diagnostic artifacts engineers used in the cited incidents but are recommended actions for your live triage.) [Data: Sources (13); Sources (30); Sources (38)] Concise recommended routing - Escalate to BU Infrastructure - Identity (Entra/Azure AD / Azure AD Connect owners) and M365/Compliance engineering immediately, and open a cross‑functional bridge with Network/Print/UC/Deskside as needed - this is the escalation pattern shown in prior, similar cases. [Data: Sources (13); Sources (30)] Key supporting records referenced - COMPLIANCE_TEAM / Azure AD Connect collision -> consolidation + full sync (engineer‑led) [Data: Sources (13)]. - Sensitivity‑label vs ACL investigations; label reapply + token invalidation performed by identity/M365 engineers (incidents with intermittent access / Incognito symptom) [Data: Sources (30)]. - SP_Project_IT nesting outage / group‑nesting correction (project folder access) [Data: Sources (20)]. - Provisioning/script causing cross‑service misassignment (voice policy) and engineer remediation (shows automation can propagate changes across services) [Data: Sources (9)]. - Token caching / Conditional Access timing used to explain intermittent access (engineers used sign‑in logs) [Data: Sources (38)]. If you want, I can now draft the single‑paragraph escalation note (paste‑ready) with the minimum artifacts and ticket links to hand to Identity/Core Engineering - that would be a recommended next action (not a record extract).

question_text:
Hi IT-Team, sorry but we have a very odd combination of issues since yesterday and multiple teams are blocked.

First: In D365 (Finance & Operations), Financial Reports is basically unusable. When we go to General Ledger -> Financial Reports -> Edit, it either errors or just spins forever. It is not only one person: several users report it. Also, older reports suddenly show the author as "unknown", and when we try to open or create a report it sometimes says the report cannot be found. This is happening in test and production, so we cannot even use test as a workaround.

Second: our new hotline numbers from the current marketing campaign are dead. Calls to the old main number still ring through, but if a customer calls one of the new ones it fails right away. The carrier insists the calls reach our edge, but they never seem to land with anyone here.

Third: the Basel office has a very specific behavior on outside calls. The call connects, people can talk briefly, and then it cuts off after roughly half a minute almost every time, like clockwork. Internal calls are fine, and colleagues at other sites are fine. Apparently something on our voice setup was changed last week.

Fourth: a few team leads say they no longer get any kind of pop-up or alert when Teams messages come in on their laptops. Messages still arrive — they just only notice them once they actually open Teams. Their phones are unaffected. Windows updates ran last week and at least one person had some focus mode turned on during a meeting.

Is this connected or just multiple incidents at once? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Recommended classification - second_level_support Why (brief, evidence‑backed) - This ticket reports simultaneous, reproducible faults across multiple platform tiers - a tenant app (D365 / Financial Reports) unusable in PROD+TEST, inbound numbers that reach the edge but never reach users, site‑specific ~30s call drops, and client notification abnormalities. Prior incidents with the same cross‑system pattern were escalated to engineering teams (identity/infra/UC/Core Engineering) rather than resolved by first‑line runbooks. For example: - High‑impact reporting/app outages required AOS/IIS/TLS and vendor escalation (engineer‑level actions) in the Management Reports / Data Mart incident [This is the documented pattern] [Data: Sources (0)]. - SBC/Direct‑Routing faults where new number ranges were missing from translation tables or where carrier delivery reached the edge but calls failed at the SBC were diagnosed and fixed by UC/System Engineers using SBC traces and translation‑table changes [This matches your "new numbers dead / carrier says it reaches the edge" symptom] [Data: Sources (8)]. - Site‑specific ~30s disconnects were previously traced to Voice‑VLAN NAT/session‑timeout or SBC/session‑timer mismatches and required network/SBC configuration changes by engineers [This mirrors your Basel symptom] [Data: Sources (16)]. - VM restore / print‑server incidents (where OS/VM/host changes caused multi‑service impact) required Core Engineering actions (host restore, share toggles, forensic collection) rather than first‑line steps [Analogy for an infrastructure‑level cause affecting multiple services] [Data: Sources (28)]. - Provisioning/automation defects that changed policy assignment (e.g., group->voice policy scripts) were corrected by Identity/Provisioning engineers, not by helpdesk alone - relevant where a group/provisioning change could explain multiple downstream failures [Data: Sources (9)]. What the indexed records explicitly document (facts you can rely on) - Multi‑tier outages that span identity/provisioning, UC/SBC, and server/host layers have required second‑level engineering escalation and hands‑on fixes in the indexed corpus [Data: Sources (0, 8, 16, 28, 9)]. - The artifacts and owners that proved decisive in those past escalations included: AOS/IIS and application server diagnostics and vendor involvement for reporting outages [Data: Sources (0)]; SBC SIP/RTP traces and translation‑table edits for number‑range reachability problems [Data: Sources (8)]; network/NAT/session‑timeout and firewall rule adjustments for ~30s drops [Data: Sources (16)]; and host/VM snapshot, spooler and network‑share manipulations for print‑server outages [Data: Sources (28)]. Provisioning script fixes were performed by Identity teams when automation misassigned policies [Data: Sources (9)]. What I am recommending (clearly marked as recommendations; not taken as performed in the records) - Recommendation: treat this ticket as second_level_support and escalate immediately to: - Application/Platform (D365/AOS/IIS) engineering to collect app server traces, AOS/IIS error logs and to validate whether a backend change or TLS/cipher/AOS regression aligns to the Financial Reports failures. - Identity/Provisioning (Azure AD / Entra / provisioning scripts) to check recent group/provisioning changes, Azure AD Connect syncs, and policy provisioning runs (because provisioning changes have caused multi‑service side effects in prior cases). - Unified Communications / SBC and Network teams to extract SBC SIP traces, RTP statistics, SBC translation tables, and firewall/NAT/session‑timeout configs for the affected carrier inbound numbers and for Basel site media path. - Core Engineering / Infrastructure to preserve any recent VM/host restore snapshots and host logs if any server/VM changes were performed in the incident window (this follows the printserver precedent). - Recommendation: collect and attach these artifacts to the escalated incident (these are recommended - they mirror the logs used in the cited past cases): D365/AOS/IIS server logs and recent deployments, Azure AD sign‑in + provisioning sync logs, Microsoft Graph group membership snapshots, SBC SIP traces and translation-table config, firewall/NAT change logs and Voice VLAN NAT timeout values, and any VM/host snapshot IDs + event logs. Concise rationale in one sentence - Because this is a simultaneous, reproducible, cross‑service outage that maps closely to previously recorded incidents requiring hands‑on engineering (application platform + identity + UC + infrastructure), the indexed precedent and artifact usage support escalation to second_level_support [Data: Sources (0, 8, 16, 28, 9)]. Top source records relied on (examples) - Management Reporting / AOS / vendor escalation (engineer work) [Data: Sources (0)] - SBC / Direct Routing number‑range & translation‑table incident (carrier->SBC reached but not delivered) [Data: Sources (8)] - Basel call‑drops tied to NAT/session timeout (≈30s BYE) [Data: Sources (16)] - SAP printserver VM‑restore / network‑share remediation (Core Engineering hands‑on) [Data: Sources (28)] - Provisioning script -> voice‑policy misassignment corrected by Identity team (automation caused telephony failures) [Data: Sources (9)] If you want I can now: - Draft the minimal, evidence‑focused escalation note to paste into your ticket (which exact artifacts to attach and which teams to notify) - this would be a recommended operational template (not itself extracted from the incident records).

question_text:
Hi IT, I need help because we have multiple problems at once and people are losing it.

In Zurich (training room), people are trying to make outside calls in Teams. The call goes through and the system shows it's active, the person on the other end can hear us fine, but on our end there's just nothing coming back — total dead air, like our side has no incoming sound at all. We already tried swapping out audio devices entirely (different mic, different speakers) and it makes no difference. Calls between people inside the company work normally.

At the same time in Basel the office is basically unable to print. One new colleague literally has no printers showing up at all on their laptop even though everyone else in the same room can print fine. Another colleague can see the printer in the list, but the moment they try to send something to it they instantly get an "access denied" message and nothing even shows up anywhere. And for the rest of us: documents go into the queue but then just sit there forever like nothing is processing.

We did the usual stuff (restart laptop, unplug dock, change USB port, try a different app) but nothing changed. Does this look connected or just bad timing, and please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Recommendation: second_level_support Reasoning (evidence-backed) - The Zurich symptom (external Teams calls "connected" but no inbound audio on site) matches prior Direct‑Routing/Media‑Bypass incidents that required SBC / network engineering and firewall/NAT changes (investigations used SBC traces, media‑port checks and firewall rule fixes). Engineers had to change SBC/translation or firewall rules to restore media in those cases [Data: Sources (23, 16, 8)]. - The Basel print symptoms (new user sees no printers; another user gets "Access denied"; jobs stuck in queues) match prior print‑service incidents that went beyond desktop runbooks - examples include missing GPO application, an explicit Deny ACE on a queue, VLAN/routing issues and even VM‑restore/share‑publishing failures that required Core Engineering or print‑server remediation (disabling/re‑enabling shares, ACL edits, network/routing fixes) [Data: Sources (28, 22, 3, 39)]. - When multiple independent subsystems fail at the same time (UC/media + printing + potential provisioning/identity implications), the indexed cases show these are typically configuration/synchronization or cross‑system faults that were escalated to engineers rather than resolved by first‑line steps [Data: Sources (13, 30)]. What the records explicitly document (short) - SBC logs and test calls were decisive to prove whether faults were translation/routing vs media/NAT; fixes required SBC/firewall tuning in prior cases [Data: Sources (23, 8)]. - Printing outages that affected many users were often rooted in AD/GPO/queue ACL or print‑server share exposure problems and in some incidents required Core Engineering actions (share toggle, VM/restore follow‑up) to clear queued jobs [Data: Sources (28, 3)]. (These are the concrete precedents I used above.) Recommended next steps (these are recommendations, not items taken from the indexed records) 1) Triage/collect evidence (attach to the ticket): concise list of affected users (samples) and exact failure timestamps (UTC if possible); Teams Admin Center call traces or per‑call correlation IDs; SBC SIP traces for sample failed calls (INVITE/200/SDP/BYE); firewall/NAT logs for the same times; print‑server queue snapshots, PrintService event log slices and queue ACLs; recent GPO / AD group‑membership changes for the affected users. 2) Escalate immediately to: Unified Communications / SBC owner + Network team (for the Zurich Teams media traces) and Core Engineering / Print services + AD/GPO owner (for Basel printing). Also include BU Infrastructure - Identity if you see recent group/sync activity. 3) Mark this ticket PRIO HIGH for cross‑team bridge/coordination - the historical pattern shows cross‑domain work (UC, Network, Print, Identity) is needed. If you want, I will draft the minimal evidence request text you can paste into the ticket for each team (SBC/Network, Print/Core, Identity) and include the exact fields/timestamps to extract (recommended).

question_text:
Hi IT, we need help asap because it feels like the whole company is falling apart since the weekend changes. I'm trying to describe it without guessing, but it's a mess.

1) In D365 (F&O) the Financial Reports / Report Designer thing is basically broken. It either loads forever or errors out. What's super strange: older reports suddenly show the author as 'unknown' and sometimes it says a report 'cannot be found' even when we used it last week. This happens in both test and prod, so we can't even use test as a workaround.

2) Sales: the desktop mail app is acting up. People can receive emails, but anything they send just sits in Outbox and later fails with a connection-style error. If they send the same email from the browser mailbox it goes through. Mobile seems fine too.

3) External partners: in Teams they are shown as Offline all day even while they are actively writing to us. Chats arrive late and sometimes calls to them do not ring. Internals are fine.

4) Zurich site: external calls in Teams connect, the other side says they hear us, but on our side it's dead silent (zero audio). We swapped headsets and speakers and it's the same. Internal calls are ok.

5) Basel site: external calls connect and then drop after about half a minute almost every time, like a timer. Internal calls are ok.

6) Also with the new marketing hotline numbers: calls to the old main number work, but calls to the new number range fail instantly. The provider claims they deliver the call to our edge.

7) Printing is chaos too, but it's not one single thing. A new employee in Basel sees literally no printers at all, even though everyone next to them can print. Another user can see the printer but gets 'Access denied' immediately when printing. And for the rest of us: jobs show up in the queue and then just sit there forever like nothing is processing.

8) One more: in Basel the main office printer sometimes shows jobs for a second and then they vanish, while other printers in the building still work.

People keep blaming laptops, docks, and Windows updates, but all of this started at basically the same time after the weekend work. Is this one connected change or just unlucky timing? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Recommendation - Classify this ticket as: second_level_support. What the indexed records explicitly document (evidence) - D365 / Financial Reports outages like "Report Designer fails, authors become 'unknown' and reports missing in test+prod" required application‑team / vendor escalation in prior incidents (Data Mart / FRDB work and Microsoft support case opened) - these were not solved by routine desk steps [Data: Sources (0); Sources (11)]. - Desktop Outlook send‑from‑Outbox while OWA/mobile works was previously tied to tenant auth policy/SMTP AUTH problems and was diagnosed from Exchange Event Log 535 entries; that case required selective Conditional Access / infrastructure action (System Engineer involvement) rather than only first‑line fixes [Data: Sources (32)]. - Teams presence/federation and partner‑offline symptoms were previously resolved by tenant/federation policy changes and required Teams/tenant‑policy engineering checks (Get‑CsTenantFederationConfiguration, Admin Center) and blueprint‑aligned fixes [Data: Sources (26)]. - One‑way audio at Zurich matched a documented site firewall/media change where outbound RTP/media port blocking caused zero audio; engineers validated with temporary port relax tests and applied a permanent firewall/SBC alignment per the Direct‑Routing blueprint [Data: Sources (23)]. - Basel ~30s call drops matched a documented Voice VLAN / NAT session‑timeout issue where SBC/session timers and NAT settings were adjusted by engineering to stop ~30s BYEs [Data: Sources (16)]. - Incoming calls to a new hotline number range failed in a prior case because the SBC translation/normalization table lacked the new range; the carrier confirmed delivery to the SIP trunk and engineers applied a temporary translation rule to validate, then applied a permanent translation/routing update [Data: Sources (8)]. - Printing incidents in the corpus range from routine GPO/gpupdate fixes to high‑impact print‑server failures: examples include simple gpupdate/map fixes and ACL removals (first‑line remedies) and a SAP printing outage where spooler restarts/VM restore/BSOD and share re‑publishing required Core Engineering / infra on‑call involvement [Data: Sources (39,27,22,28,29)]. Why this ticket maps to second_level_support - The current ticket describes simultaneous, cross‑domain failures across application (D365), mail/SMTP auth, Teams federation/media (site‑specific media and timer symptoms), SBC/trunk translation, and multi‑site printing. In the indexed knowledge base the same pattern (multiple domains affected at once) consistently required specialist engineering teams (App Ops/Microsoft, Unified Communications / SBC, Network/Firewall, Core Engineering for print servers). Examples above show these were handled as engineering escalations, not routine first‑line-only cases [Data: Sources (0); Sources (32); Sources (23,16,8); Sources (28,29)]. - The records also show that single‑domain printing/GPO issues could be first‑line (gpupdate, queue unpause), but when printing failures coincide with server restores/BSODs or with broader tenant/perimeter changes the incidents were escalated [Data: Sources (39,27); Sources (28,29)]. What the records do NOT prove (important limitation) - The indexed incidents do NOT contain a single definitive record proving every one of these symptoms in one tenant was caused by one single change applied to all systems in the same weekend. Prior cases document distinct change events that temporally overlapped (firewall/WAF hardening, NAT/session timeout edits, SBC translation omissions, tenant policy changes) rather than one universal root cause across every symptom [Data: Sources (23,16,8,26)]. Recommended next steps (these are recommendations - they are not stated as prior actions in the records) - Escalate immediately to second‑level and open a cross‑team incident bridge that includes: D365/App Ops, Exchange/Identity, Unified Communications/SBC, Network/Firewall, and Print/Core Engineering. (recommended) - Collect and preserve evidence used in similar incidents: SBC SIP traces and Call‑IDs, carrier CDRs for the new hotline range, Teams call diagnostics and firewall change diffs, Exchange Event Log 535 entries and Message Trace, D365 server/FRDB logs and the Microsoft support package, and print‑server event logs / crash dumps and recent VM/restore metadata. (recommended) Primary supporting tickets / records used - D365 / Data Mart & Microsoft escalation example [Data: Sources (0); Sources (11)] - Exchange SMTP AUTH / Outlook Outbox case [Data: Sources (32)] - Zurich one‑way audio (firewall/media port blocking) [Data: Sources (23)] - Basel ~30s drops (NAT/session timeout) [Data: Sources (16)] - New number‑range / SBC translation omission (carrier delivered to trunk; SBC answered 404; temporary rule validated fix) [Data: Sources (8)] - Printing: gpupdate/GPO and paused‑queue fixes vs SAP printserver VM restore/BSOD requiring Core Engineering [Data: Sources (39,27,22,28,29)] Bottom line - The symptom set and the precedent in the indexed records point to an infrastructure/engineering‑level problem spanning multiple domains. Classify and escalate this as second_level_support and engage the specialist teams above for coordinated log collection and rapid remediation.
